section.title: The Deserted City
section.sectionRationale: The book opens with a funeral dirge over Jerusalem. The first acrostic poem walks slowly through the ruined city: a widow who was once a queen, roads that mourn because no one comes to the feasts, and a people led away captive. Halfway through, the voice shifts from a narrator describing Zion to Zion herself speaking, owning her sin and crying out to the LORD.
section.thesis: Jerusalem sits deserted and disgraced because of her own transgressions; yet even in her ruin she turns from her useless lovers to the LORD, confesses that He is righteous, and pleads with Him to look on her affliction.
section.frameNote: Two halves. First, the narrator surveys the deserted city, its emptiness, its shame, and the hand of the LORD behind it, 1:1 to 11. Then Zion herself speaks, confessing her rebellion, owning that God is righteous, and begging Him to see her sorrow, 1:12 to 22.

units.label: The city sits alone
units.note: a widow who was once a queen

verses.text: {{eykah|How}} lonely lies the city, once so full of people! She who was great among the nations has become a widow. The princess of the provinces has become a slave.
She weeps aloud in the night, with tears upon her cheeks. Among all her lovers there is no one to {{nacham|comfort}} her. All her friends have betrayed her; they have become her enemies.
Judah has gone into exile under affliction and harsh slavery; she dwells among the nations but finds no place to rest. All her pursuers have overtaken her in the midst of her distress.
The roads to Zion mourn, because no one comes to her appointed feasts. All her gates are deserted; her priests groan, her maidens grieve, and she herself is bitter with anguish.
Her foes have become her masters; her enemies are at ease. For the LORD has brought her grief because of her many {{pesha|transgressions}}. Her children have gone away as captives before the enemy.
All the splendor has departed from the Daughter of Zion. Her princes are like deer that find no pasture; they lack the strength to flee in the face of the hunter.

units.label: Her shame and the LORD's hand

verses.text: In the days of her affliction and wandering Jerusalem remembers all the treasures that were hers in days of old. When her people fell into enemy hands she received no help. Her enemies looked upon her, laughing at her downfall.
Jerusalem has sinned greatly; therefore she has become an object of scorn. All who honored her now despise her, for they have seen her nakedness; she herself groans and turns away.
Her uncleanness stains her skirts; she did not consider her end. Her downfall was astounding; there was no one to comfort her. Look, O LORD, on my affliction, for the enemy has triumphed!
The adversary has seized all her treasures. For she has seen the nations enter her sanctuary, those You had forbidden to enter Your assembly.
All her people groan as they search for bread. They have traded their treasures for food to keep themselves alive. Look, O LORD, and consider, for I have become despised.

units.label: Zion speaks: is there any sorrow like mine?
units.note: the day of His fierce anger

verses.text: Is this nothing to you, all you who pass by? Look around and see! Is there any sorrow like mine, which was inflicted on me, which the LORD made me suffer on the day of His fierce anger?
He sent fire from on high, and it overpowered my bones. He spread a net for my feet and turned me back. He made me desolate, faint all the day long.

My {{pesha|transgressions}} are bound into a yoke, knit together by His hand; they are draped over my neck, and the Lord has broken my strength. He has delivered me into the hands of those I cannot withstand.
The Lord has rejected all the mighty men in my midst; He has summoned an army against me to crush my young warriors. Like grapes in a winepress, the Lord has trampled the Virgin Daughter of Judah.
For these things I weep; my eyes flow with tears. For there is no one nearby to {{nacham|comfort}} me, no one to revive my soul. My children are destitute because the enemy has prevailed.
Zion stretches out her hands, but there is no one to comfort her. The LORD has decreed against Jacob that his neighbors become his foes. Jerusalem has become an unclean thing among them.

units.label: The LORD is righteous: a plea for justice

verses.text: The LORD is righteous, yet I rebelled against His command. Listen, all you people; look upon my suffering. My young men and maidens have gone into captivity.
I called out to my lovers, but they have betrayed me. My priests and elders perished in the city while they searched for food to keep themselves alive.
See, O LORD, how distressed I am! I am churning within; my heart is pounding within me, for I have been most rebellious. Outside, the sword bereaves; inside, there is death.

People have heard my groaning, but there is no one to comfort me. All my enemies have heard of my trouble; they are glad that You have caused it. May You bring the day You have announced, so that they may become like me.
Let all their wickedness come before You, and deal with them as You have dealt with me because of all my {{pesha|transgressions}}. For my groans are many, and my heart is faint.

eykah.gloss: How, or Alas. This single word of stunned grief opens the poem and gives the book its Hebrew name. It is the cry of a mourner at a graveside, staring at ruin and asking how it could come to this.

nacham.gloss: To comfort or console. The refrain there is no one to comfort her runs through the chapter like a bell tolling. Every human comforter has failed, and the ache of the whole book is the longing for a comfort that only God can finally give.

pesha.gloss: Transgression, rebellion, a deliberate breaking with rightful authority. It is stronger than a mere mistake: it names sin as revolt against the LORD. Zion does not blame fate or her enemies alone; she confesses that her many transgressions are why she suffers.

basic.context: Lamentations opens like a funeral song sung over a dead body, except the body is a city. Jerusalem has been destroyed by Babylon in 586 BC, the temple burned, the people dragged into exile. The poet walks through the empty streets and weeps: the city that was once a queen among the nations is now a widow, alone, betrayed by her allies, with no one to comfort her. Then, in the middle, the city herself begins to speak. She does not deny her guilt. She admits that the LORD is righteous and that her own rebellion brought this down. And she keeps crying out for God to look and see.
basic.hermeneutics: This is real grief put into careful poetry, an acrostic that runs through the Hebrew alphabet, as if to say the sorrow is complete, from A to Z. It is important to read it as honest lament, not despair without God. The city grieves and confesses in the same breath. The key question is: how do God's people mourn when the disaster is deserved? The answer here is to weep truthfully, own the sin, refuse the false comforters, and turn the whole crushing weight of it toward the LORD.
basic.originalLanguages: The book is named for its first word, eykah, How, or Alas, the gasp of a mourner. Again and again we hear there is no one to comfort her, from the root nacham, and that aching for comfort is the pulse of the book. The reason for the ruin is named plainly with the word pesha, transgression, which means rebellion, not just a slip. Zion owns her rebellion even as she grieves its cost.
basic.typology: The deserted city, struck under the anger of God for sin, points forward to the true and greater grief of the cross, where Jesus bears the wrath our sin deserves. The cry Is there any sorrow like mine has long been heard by the church on the lips of the suffering Christ. And the longing for a comforter is answered when Jesus promises the Comforter, the Spirit, and when God finally wipes away every tear.
basic.christ: Jerusalem's cry, Is there any sorrow like mine, is answered most deeply not by the city but by Christ, the man of sorrows, who enters the God forsaken place we deserved. Jesus wept over this same Jerusalem. He bears the fierce anger of God against transgression so that His people might be comforted. Where Zion had no one to comfort her, Christ becomes our comfort and sends the Comforter.
basic.god: God is holy and just: the destruction is not random, it is His righteous response to persistent rebellion, and Zion says so plainly, the LORD is righteous. Yet He is not absent. He is the One being cried out to, again and again, Look, O LORD. Even the appeal for Him to see is an act of faith that He is there and that He cares.
basic.crossReferences: Jeremiah 52 records the fall of Jerusalem this poem mourns. Luke 19 shows Jesus weeping over the city. Isaiah 53 gives us the man of sorrows who bears our grief. John 14 promises the Comforter, and Revelation 21 promises the day when God wipes away every tear.
basic.oneStory: This is the low point of the Old Testament story: the city of God in ruins because of sin, exactly as the covenant warned. Yet the honest grief and confession here already reach toward the LORD, and the whole Bible answers the cry for a comforter in Jesus, the man of sorrows who bears the judgment and turns mourning into joy.

context.literary: The first of five poems, and a carefully built acrostic: its twenty two verses each begin with a successive letter of the Hebrew alphabet, giving ordered form to overwhelming grief. It divides in two: a narrator describes the desolate city, 1:1 to 11, and then Zion herself speaks in the first person, 1:12 to 22, with brief cries breaking through even in the first half.
context.historical: The poem mourns the fall of Jerusalem to Babylon in 586 BC, when the city was besieged, starved, captured, and burned, and the people carried into exile. The lovers who betray her are the foreign allies Judah trusted for protection instead of trusting the LORD. Famine, the sword, and captivity are described as recent, raw memory.
context.canonical: Lamentations stands as the fulfillment of the covenant curses warned in Leviticus 26 and Deuteronomy 28 for persistent covenant breaking. It gives Israel, and the church, inspired words for grieving loss under the discipline of God, and its longing for comfort reaches forward to the gospel.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To lament the fall of Jerusalem truthfully and fully, to confess that the catastrophe is the just judgment of a righteous God on real rebellion, and to model turning that grief toward the LORD in appeal rather than toward despair or blame.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The poem describes the ruin and Zion's suffering; it does not prescribe the disaster as a pattern to seek. What it models, and so commends, is honest lament, true confession of sin, refusal of false comforters, and persistent appeal to God even under deserved discipline.

debates.question: Is the destruction presented as deserved judgment, or as suffering beyond what was fair?

views.label: Deserved judgment for sin
views.case: The poem states it directly: the LORD has brought her grief because of her many transgressions, 1:5, and the LORD is righteous, yet I rebelled, 1:18. Zion owns the justice of what has fallen on her.

views.label: Grief that also protests the severity
views.case: The raw cries, Is there any sorrow like mine, 1:12, and the appeals for God to look and to repay the enemies, show the poem is not a cool verdict but a wounded plea that also asks God to act against the cruelty of the oppressors.

debates.bearing: Both are true at once and that is the point: the suffering is genuinely deserved and genuinely agonizing, and biblical lament holds honest confession and honest anguish together before God rather than choosing one.

debates.question: Who are the lovers who betray her in 1:2 and 1:19?

views.label: Foreign allies and nations
views.case: The lovers are the surrounding powers Judah courted for military help, Egypt and others, who failed her in the crisis and became her enemies.

views.label: The idols and false gods she pursued
views.case: The prophets repeatedly picture Israel's turning to other gods as spiritual adultery, so the lovers also evoke the idols that promised security and gave none.

debates.bearing: Whether political or spiritual, the point stands: everything Zion trusted instead of the LORD abandoned her, and only the God she rebelled against remains as the One who can hear her cry.

study.originalLanguages: The poem opens with eykah, How, the mourner's gasp that becomes the book's Hebrew title, a word better felt than defined. Its deepest ache is carried by the root nacham, to comfort: five times we hear there is no one to comfort her, and that emptiness cries out for the comfort only God gives. And the honesty of the poem turns on pesha, transgression, a word for deliberate rebellion against rightful authority. Zion does not merely regret consequences; she confesses revolt against the LORD, and only then does her plea for mercy ring true.

typology.type: The deserted city under God's fierce anger
typology.fulfillment: Christ on the cross, forsaken and bearing the wrath against sin, so that His people are never finally forsaken.

typology.type: Is there any sorrow like mine, 1:12
typology.fulfillment: The man of sorrows, Isaiah 53, whose grief in bearing our sin surpasses every other, long read by the church on the lips of the suffering Christ.
Jesus who comforts the mourning and sends the Comforter, the Spirit, John 14, until God wipes away every tear, Revelation 21.

study.christ: The cry that pierces this chapter, Is there any sorrow like mine, which the LORD made me suffer on the day of His fierce anger, finds its truest voice in Jesus. He wept over this very city, Luke 19, and then went outside its gate to bear the fierce anger of God against transgression in Himself, the man of sorrows acquainted with grief. Where Zion searched in vain for a comforter, Christ is the comfort of His people and the giver of the Comforter. He enters the deserted, God forsaken place that our rebellion earned, so that we might be gathered in and consoled.
study.god: God is unmistakably righteous here: the poem insists the ruin is His just response to real and repeated rebellion, and Zion herself declares the LORD is righteous. Yet He is never treated as gone. He is the One addressed in every cry, Look, O LORD, on my affliction. His discipline is severe but it is fatherly, aimed not at abandonment but at repentance, and the very act of crying to Him assumes He is present, listening, and able to save.

crossReferences.note: The historical account of the burning of Jerusalem and the temple that this poem mourns.
The covenant curse of siege, famine, and exile for disobedience, now fallen on Judah.
Jesus weeps over Jerusalem and foretells another desolation, echoing this lament.
The man of sorrows who bears our grief, the deeper answer to Is there any sorrow like mine.
The promise that God will wipe away every tear, the end of all lament.

study.oneStory: This chapter is the covenant's warning come true: the city of God laid waste because of persistent sin, the darkest hour of Israel's story. Yet the grief is not godless. Zion confesses that the LORD is righteous and keeps crying out to Him for comfort. The whole Bible answers that cry in Jesus, the man of sorrows, who bears the fierce anger her sin deserved and becomes the comfort she could not find, until the day every tear is wiped away.

quiz.q: What kind of writing is Lamentations 1?

options.text: A funeral dirge over the fallen city, shaped as an alphabetical acrostic of grief.
options.note: The poem mourns Jerusalem verse by verse through the Hebrew alphabet, ordered grief from A to Z.

options.text: A poem of sorrow over Jerusalem's destruction.
options.note: True to its content, though it misses the deliberate acrostic form that structures the lament.

options.text: A record of laws for rebuilding the temple.
options.note: It is grief and confession, not legal instruction.

options.text: A victory song celebrating Babylon's triumph.
options.note: It weeps over the ruin; it does not celebrate the conqueror.

quiz.q: Why does the poem say the disaster came upon Jerusalem?

options.text: Because of her many transgressions; the LORD is righteous and she rebelled against His command.
options.note: The poem names sin plainly, 1:5 and 1:18, and Zion confesses it.

options.text: Because God brought grief on her for her sin.
options.note: Correct, though it leaves out Zion's own confession that the LORD is righteous.

options.text: Because her enemies were simply stronger than her.
options.note: Babylon was the instrument, but the poem traces the cause to the LORD's judgment on sin.

options.text: Because God had forgotten His covenant with her.
options.note: The ruin fulfills the covenant warnings; God has not forgotten but has kept His word.

quiz.q: What refrain runs through the chapter like a tolling bell?

options.text: There is no one to comfort her; every human comforter has failed.
options.note: The word nacham, comfort, recurs, and its absence is the ache of the whole book.

options.text: That Zion weeps and finds no one to help her.
options.note: A fair paraphrase, though the specific, repeated cry is the lack of a comforter.

options.text: That the harvest had failed across the land.
options.note: Famine is present, but the repeated refrain is the absence of comfort.

options.text: That the enemies were about to repent.
options.note: The enemies gloat; there is no hint of their repentance.

quiz.q: How does Zion respond in the second half of the poem, 1:12 to 22?

options.text: She speaks for herself, confesses her rebellion, calls the LORD righteous, and pleads with Him to see her sorrow.
options.note: The voice shifts to Zion, who owns her guilt and turns her grief toward God.

options.text: She cries out to God about her suffering and her sin.
options.note: True, and more precisely she both confesses guilt and appeals for God to look and act.

options.text: She curses God and turns away from Him entirely.
options.note: She laments bitterly but keeps turning toward the LORD, not away from Him.

options.text: She declares that she has done nothing wrong.
options.note: She confesses she rebelled and that the LORD is righteous.

quiz.q: How does this chapter point forward to Christ?

options.text: Its cry, Is there any sorrow like mine, and its longing for a comforter are answered in Jesus, the man of sorrows who bears God's anger and comforts His people.
options.note: Christ enters the forsaken place and becomes the comfort Zion could not find.

options.text: Jesus is the suffering one who grieves and brings comfort.
options.note: True, and the specific links are the man of sorrows bearing wrath and giving the Comforter.

options.text: It teaches that Jerusalem would never be inhabited again.
options.note: The poem mourns the present ruin; its forward reach is to Christ, not to permanent desolation.

options.text: It has no connection to Christ at all.
options.note: The New Testament reads the man of sorrows and the Comforter as the answer to this grief.

questions: The poem begins with a single stunned word, How. When has grief left you without words? What does it mean that God gives His people inspired songs for exactly those moments?

Zion had many lovers, allies and idols she trusted, and every one of them betrayed her in the crisis. What are you leaning on for security instead of the LORD, and what happens to it when trouble comes?
The hardest honesty in this chapter is the confession the LORD is righteous, yet I rebelled. Where do you need to stop blaming circumstances or others and own your own sin before God?
Five times we hear there is no one to comfort her. Where are you aching for a comfort that no person or possession has been able to give, and how does Jesus meet that ache?
Even while grieving, Zion keeps crying Look, O LORD. How might turning your sorrow into honest prayer, rather than silence or despair, change the way you carry a hard season?

translationNotes.note: How translates eykah, the mourner's cry that opens the poem and gives the book its Hebrew name; it carries stunned grief more than a simple question.
The clause rendered they have seen her nakedness can also be rendered they have seen her shame; either way it pictures Jerusalem's disgrace laid bare before onlookers.
The BSB source prints an em dash after sanctuary; it is rendered here as a clean break with a comma, with no change to the wording.
Most Hebrew manuscripts read my transgressions are bound into a yoke; some manuscripts and the Septuagint read He kept watch over my sins. The confession of guilt stands either way.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Lamentations 1:1-22. Verse segments concatenated in order and separated by single spaces; poetic lines rendered as flowing prose within each verse. The em dash after sanctuary in 1:10 was converted to a clean break with a comma. No em or en dashes used anywhere.
